title: Housing Fairness Act of 2025
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.
plain_summary: The Housing Fairness Act of 2025 requires the government to test for housing discrimination across the country by checking if people are treated differently when trying to rent or buy homes because of their race, religion, disability, or other protected characteristics. The bill gives more money to programs that help people who face housing discrimination and continues funding these programs through 2028. It also creates new grants for organizations to study why housing discrimination happens and to test new ways to prevent it. The goal is to make sure all people are treated fairly when looking for housing.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.
subjects: Department of Housing and Urban Development

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Veterans, Women, Families with Children, Race, and Persons with Disabilities Housing Fairness Act of two thousand twenty five or the Housing Fairness Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Testing for Discrimination
sections.text: The Secretary of Housing and Urban Development must run a nationwide program to test for housing discrimination. This testing will find and record differences in how people are treated when they try to rent or buy homes or get home loans. The testing will look for unfair treatment based on race, color, religion, sex, family status (having children), disability status, or national origin. The testing will also measure how common these unfair practices are across the housing and lending markets. The Secretary must work with qualified fair housing organizations to do this testing. These are organizations that are defined in existing housing law as having the right qualifications to enforce fair housing rules. The Secretary must tell Congress how well this program is working. The Secretary must also create rules that require each application for this program to include a description of the activities the applicant plans to do, a description of the applicant's experience with fair housing programs, and a description of how the applicant will evaluate the results of their work. The Secretary must report to Congress every two years about the overall results of this testing along with recommendations for new laws or actions to fix any problems found. The Secretary must also report to Congress every year with a detailed summary of messages received through the Office of Fair Housing's twenty four hour phone line, email, and website. The Secretary can include these reports as part of other required fair housing reports. The results of this testing can be used by the Secretary, other federal agencies, state and local governments, or nonprofit organizations to start investigations or take legal action against housing discrimination found through the testing. In this section, disability status means the same thing as handicap in existing civil rights law, and familial status means the same thing as defined in existing civil rights law. This section does not change any criminal laws or the Truth in Lending Act. Within one hundred eighty days of this law being passed, the Secretary must create rules that set minimum training standards for people who do the testing. These rules will be used to evaluate the testers and must be created with public notice and comment. Congress approves spending fifteen million dollars for each year from two thousand twenty four through two thousand twenty eight to carry out this section.

sections.title: Section 3 Increase in Funding for the Fair Housing Initiatives Program
sections.text: This section changes an existing housing law to increase funding for fair housing programs. The changes require that private nonprofit fair housing organizations must be qualified organizations. The section increases the amount of money Congress approves for these programs to forty two million five hundred thousand dollars for each year from two thousand twenty four through two thousand twenty eight. At least seventy five percent of this money must go to private enforcement programs. No more than ten percent can go to education and outreach programs. Any remaining money can be used for other program activities allowed under this section. Any money approved under this section stays available until it is spent. The section also adds websites and other media outlets to the list of ways education and outreach programs can reach people. It changes the law so that only nonprofit entities can receive certain types of funding. Within one hundred eighty days of this law being passed, the Secretary must create rules that set minimum training standards for testers in organizations that get money from this program. These rules will be used to evaluate the testers and must be created with public notice and comment.

sections.title: Section 4 Sense of Congress
sections.text: Congress believes that the Secretary of Housing and Urban Development should fully follow the requirements of existing law to create and maintain a national education and outreach program that provides a coordinated effort to develop and share information about fair housing rights for people who want to rent, buy, sell, or help sell homes. The Secretary should spend all the money approved for these education and outreach programs. The Secretary should create rules about fair housing obligations for anyone who receives federal housing and community development money, requiring them to actively promote fair housing as defined in existing civil rights law. The Secretary should also fully follow the requirements of existing fair housing law.

sections.title: Section 5 Grants to Private Entities to Study Housing Discrimination
sections.text: The Secretary of Housing and Urban Development must run a competitive matching grant program to help public and private nonprofit organizations conduct comprehensive studies and pilot projects related to housing discrimination. The studies can examine the causes of housing discrimination and segregation, the effects of housing discrimination and segregation on education, poverty, and economic development, or the incidents, causes, and effects of housing discrimination and segregation on veterans and military personnel. The pilot projects should test solutions that will help prevent or reduce housing discrimination and segregation. To be eligible for a grant, an organization must submit an application with information required by the Secretary, agree to provide matching non federal funds for fifty percent of the total grant amount (which can include donated items), and either meet the requirements of a qualified fair housing enforcement organization or work with such an organization as a primary subcontractor. The Secretary must report to Congress every two years with a detailed summary of the results of these studies and pilot projects, along with recommendations for new laws or actions to address any issues found. The Secretary can include these reports as part of other required fair housing reports. Congress approves spending five million dollars for each year from two thousand twenty four through two thousand twenty eight to carry out this section.

sections.title: Section 6 Limitation on Use of Funds
sections.text: None of the money made available under this law or the changes made by this law can be used for political activities, political advocacy, or lobbying as defined by federal budget guidelines for nonprofit organizations. The money also cannot be used for travel expenses to engage in political activities or for preparing or giving advice on tax returns.
